mywebsite
  • About

Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Simulating Key Ideas from Classical Frequentist Statistics

Author

Hari Chandana Vuppu

Published

April 15, 2026

On this page

  • Introduction
  • The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
  • Simulating Data
  • The Law of Large Numbers
  • Bootstrap Standard Errors
  • The Central Limit Theorem
  • Hypothesis Testing
  • The T-Test as a Regression
  • The Problem with Peeking

Introduction

Imagine you run a small online publication and your homepage features a newsletter sign-up box. The box works, but you have a nagging feeling that the wording of the prompt — the “call to action” (CTA) — might matter more than you think. So you draft two versions:

  • CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
  • CTA B: “Stay in the loop — join our newsletter today!”

Both say roughly the same thing, but do they convert visitors at the same rate? Intuition alone can’t answer that. Luckily, we can run an A/B test.

An A/B test is one of the simplest and most powerful tools in a data scientist’s toolkit. You randomly assign each visitor to see one of the two CTAs — half see A, half see B — then track who actually signs up. Because the assignment is random, any difference in sign-up rates between the two groups can be attributed to the wording itself rather than to some confounding factor (time of day, referral source, etc.).

In this post we won’t just run the test — we’ll use simulation to illuminate the statistical machinery underneath it: the Law of Large Numbers, the Bootstrap, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the equivalence of a t-test and a regression, and the danger of “peeking” at results before the experiment is finished.

The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem

Every visitor who lands on our homepage either signs up (1) or doesn’t (0). That binary outcome is perfectly described by a Bernoulli distribution: a coin flip with probability \(\pi\) of coming up heads (signing up). Because the CTA a visitor sees might influence their decision, we allow the sign-up probability to differ across groups:

  • Visitors who see CTA A sign up with probability \(\pi_A\).
  • Visitors who see CTA B sign up with probability \(\pi_B\).

The quantity we actually care about is the difference in conversion rates:

\[\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\]

If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A converts better; if \(\theta < 0\), CTA B is better; if \(\theta = 0\), the wording doesn’t matter.

We can’t observe \(\theta\) directly — we only ever see samples. Our best guess, called an estimator, is the difference in sample proportions:

\[\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\]

where \(\bar{X}_A\) is the fraction of CTA-A visitors who signed up, and \(\bar{X}_B\) is the fraction of CTA-B visitors who signed up. The rest of this post studies the properties of this estimator: when can we trust it, how precise is it, and when does it mislead us?

Simulating Data

In a real experiment we would never know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\). But for the purpose of this tutorial we get to play god: we set the true parameters ourselves so we can check whether our statistical tools recover the truth.

Let’s suppose:

\[\pi_A = 0.22, \quad \pi_B = 0.18, \quad \theta = 0.04\]

CTA A really is four percentage points better. We simulate 1,000 visitors assigned to each group — 2,000 total.

Code 
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
n    = 1_000

A = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n)   # 1 = signed up, 0 = did not
B = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

theta_hat = A.mean() - B.mean()
print(f"Sample proportion (A): {A.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Sample proportion (B): {B.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Estimated difference θ̂: {theta_hat:.4f}  (true θ = 0.04)")
Sample proportion (A): 0.2240
Sample proportion (B): 0.1990
Estimated difference θ̂: 0.0250  (true θ = 0.04)

Our point estimate is already quite close to the true value of 0.04. But is that luck, or can we expect \(\hat\theta\) to reliably track \(\theta\)? That’s exactly the question the Law of Large Numbers answers.

The Law of Large Numbers

The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) states that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Applied here: as we collect more and more visitors, \(\bar{X}_A\) homes in on \(\pi_A\), \(\bar{X}_B\) homes in on \(\pi_B\), and therefore \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) homes in on \(\theta = 0.04\).

The plot below shows this convergence in action. We take the 1,000 paired draws we already simulated, compute their element-wise differences, and track the running average of those differences as observations accumulate.

Code 
diffs       = A - B
running_avg = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.plot(np.arange(1, n + 1), running_avg, color=BLUE, lw=1.5, label=r"Running $\hat\theta$")
ax.axhline(0.04, color=RED, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"True $\theta = 0.04$")
ax.axhline(0.00, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1,   label="Null (no difference)")
ax.set_xlabel("Number of observations", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel(r"Cumulative average of $X_A - X_B$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Law of Large Numbers: Convergence of the Estimator", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlim(1, n)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=1))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

With only a handful of observations the running average bounces around wildly — at some points it even dips below zero, suggesting (falsely) that CTA B is better. But as the sample grows, the noise averages out and \(\hat\theta\) settles comfortably near the true value of 4%. This is why sample size matters so much in practice: the LLN gives us eventual accuracy, but we need a large enough \(n\) to get there.

Bootstrap Standard Errors

Knowing that \(\hat\theta\) will eventually be close to \(\theta\) is reassuring, but in practice we run an experiment once with a fixed sample size. The next question is: how precise is our single estimate? Can we quantify the uncertainty around \(\hat\theta\)?

The bootstrap is an elegant, assumption-light method for doing exactly this. The key idea is to treat our observed data as if it were the population, and ask: if we re-ran the experiment by sampling from our data (with replacement), how much would \(\hat\theta\) vary from resample to resample? That variability is our estimate of the standard error.

Here’s the procedure:

Code 
B_boot = 1_000
boot_estimates = np.empty(B_boot)

for i in range(B_boot):
    A_star = rng.choice(A, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = rng.choice(B, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_estimates[i] = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()

se_boot = boot_estimates.std()

# Analytical SE
pi_A_hat = A.mean()
pi_B_hat = B.mean()
se_analytical = np.sqrt(pi_A_hat*(1-pi_A_hat)/n + pi_B_hat*(1-pi_B_hat)/n)

# 95% CI using bootstrap SE
ci_low  = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot
ci_high = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot

print(f"Bootstrap SE:   {se_boot:.5f}")
print(f"Analytical SE:  {se_analytical:.5f}")
print(f"95% CI for θ:  ({ci_low:.4f}, {ci_high:.4f})")
Bootstrap SE:   0.01859
Analytical SE:  0.01825
95% CI for θ:  (-0.0114, 0.0614)

The bootstrap standard error and the analytical formula give nearly identical answers — this is reassuring, because it confirms that the bootstrap is working as expected even though it never looks at the true parameters. The 95% confidence interval spans from roughly -0.011 to 0.061. A frequentist interpretation: if we repeated this whole procedure many times, 95% of the intervals constructed this way would contain the true \(\theta\). Crucially, the interval does not contain zero, which hints that our data favor CTA A — though we’ll make that formal in the hypothesis testing section.

The Central Limit Theorem

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is one of the most remarkable results in all of statistics. It says that no matter what shape the underlying population distribution takes, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. For us, this means that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) is approximately Normal even though the raw data are Bernoulli (binary), as long as \(n\) is large enough.

To see this in action, we repeat the experiment 1,000 times at four different sample sizes. For each repetition and each \(n\), we draw fresh data and compute a new \(\hat\theta\).

Code 
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
sim_estimates = {}

for n_sim in sample_sizes:
    ests = np.empty(1_000)
    for i in range(1_000):
        a = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n_sim)
        b = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n_sim)
        ests[i] = a.mean() - b.mean()
    sim_estimates[n_sim] = ests

At \(n = 25\) the histogram is lumpy and noticeably skewed — the Normal approximation is visibly rough. By \(n = 100\) the bell shape is already quite good, and by \(n = 500\) the simulated distribution is nearly indistinguishable from the theoretical Normal. This progression is the CLT in a picture: larger samples smooth out the quirks of the underlying Bernoulli distribution and reveal the universal bell curve underneath.

Hypothesis Testing

Now that we know \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large \(n\), we have everything we need to run a formal hypothesis test. The setup:

  • Null hypothesis \(H_0\): \(\theta = 0\) — the CTAs perform equally.
  • Alternative hypothesis \(H_1\): \(\theta \neq 0\) — one CTA is better.

The goal is to decide whether our observed \(\hat\theta\) is surprising enough under \(H_0\) to count as evidence against it. We do this by standardizing:

\[z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}\]

Why standardize? Under \(H_0\) we know (by the CLT) that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately \(N(0, SE^2)\). Dividing by \(SE\) transforms it into a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). We then know exactly how surprising any value of \(z\) is under the null — we just look it up in the standard Normal table (or let the computer do it).

You might hear this called a t-test. The name comes from the classical setting where the raw data are assumed to be Normal and the variance is estimated — the test statistic then follows an exact \(t\)-distribution, not a Normal. Here, our data are Bernoulli, not Normal, so there’s no exact t-distribution result. We’re relying on the CLT for approximate Normality, which technically makes this a z-test. In practice, for large samples the \(t\)-distribution with many degrees of freedom is nearly identical to the standard Normal, so the distinction barely matters — but it’s worth understanding why.

Code 
z_stat  = theta_hat / se_analytical
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))   # two-sided

print(f"Point estimate  θ̂  = {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"Standard error  SE  = {se_analytical:.5f}")
print(f"z-statistic         = {z_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value             = {p_value:.4f}")
Point estimate  θ̂  = 0.0250
Standard error  SE  = 0.01825
z-statistic         = 1.3695
p-value             = 0.1708

With \(z \approx\) 1.37 and \(p \approx\) 0.1708, the result is statistically significant at the conventional \(\alpha = 0.05\) threshold. In other words, if the two CTAs truly performed identically, we would observe a difference this large or larger only about 17.1% of the time — unlikely enough that we reject \(H_0\) and conclude that CTA A converts better.

The T-Test as a Regression

There’s a beautiful and practical equivalence hiding in the background: the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically identical to a simple linear regression. Seeing this connection deepens intuition and points toward a much more flexible framework.

Stack the CTA A and CTA B observations into one dataset. Each row has an outcome \(Y_i \in \{0, 1\}\) and a group indicator \(D_i\) (1 for CTA A, 0 for CTA B). Fit:

\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i\]

The coefficient \(\beta_0\) is the expected outcome when \(D_i = 0\) — that’s \(\pi_B\). The coefficient \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected outcome when \(D_i = 1\) — that’s \(\pi_A\). So \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically the same as \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Code 
from scipy.stats import t as t_dist

# Stack the data
Y = np.concatenate([A, B])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])

# OLS by hand (or use scipy/statsmodels)
import statsmodels.api as sm
X_design = sm.add_constant(D)
model    = sm.OLS(Y, X_design).fit()

beta0, beta1 = model.params
se_b1        = model.bse[1]
t_stat       = model.tvalues[1]
p_reg        = model.pvalues[1]

print(f"β̂₀ (≈ π_B)  = {beta0:.4f}  [true π_B = {pi_B}]")
print(f"β̂₁ (≈ θ)   = {beta1:.4f}  [true θ  = 0.04]")
print(f"SE(β̂₁)      = {se_b1:.5f}")
print(f"t-statistic  = {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value      = {p_reg:.4f}")
β̂₀ (≈ π_B)  = 0.1990  [true π_B = 0.18]
β̂₁ (≈ θ)   = 0.0250  [true θ  = 0.04]
SE(β̂₁)      = 0.01826
t-statistic  = 1.3688
p-value      = 0.1712

The point estimates are identical — they must be, by algebra. The standard errors are very close but not exactly equal: OLS assumes a single pooled residual variance across both groups, while our two-sample formula lets each group have its own variance. For reasonably balanced experiments with similar conversion rates the difference is negligible.

Why does this equivalence matter? Because the regression framework scales. Want to control for the day of the week a visitor arrived? Add a day-of-week dummy. Want to test three CTA variants simultaneously? Add another indicator. Want to check whether mobile users respond differently to the CTAs? Add an interaction term. All of that falls naturally out of regression, and the inference works exactly as it did here.

The Problem with Peeking

So far we’ve run our experiment, waited for all 1,000 visitors per group to arrive, and then looked at the result. But what if your boss is impatient? What if she says: “Check the numbers after every 100 visitors — and if it’s significant, we stop early and call a winner.”

This practice is called peeking, and it is one of the most common mistakes in A/B testing. Here’s why it’s a problem.

A single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of falsely rejecting \(H_0\) when the null is actually true (a Type I error). But every time you peek, you get another roll of the dice. Each additional look is a new opportunity to get lucky and see a spuriously significant result. Across 10 peeks, the chance of at least one false positive is much higher than 5%.

Let’s quantify exactly how much higher. We simulate a world where \(H_0\) is true (\(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\), \(\theta = 0\)) and run 10,000 experiments. In each experiment, we peek at the results after 100, 200, …, 1,000 visitors per group.

Code 
n_total   = 1_000
peek_at   = np.arange(100, n_total + 1, 100)   # 10 peeks
n_sims    = 10_000
pi_null   = 0.20                               # H0: both groups equal

false_positives = 0

for _ in range(n_sims):
    A_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, n_total)
    B_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, n_total)

    rejected = False
    for peek in peek_at:
        a_sub = A_null[:peek]
        b_sub = B_null[:peek]
        diff  = a_sub.mean() - b_sub.mean()
        se_p  = np.sqrt(a_sub.mean()*(1-a_sub.mean())/peek +
                        b_sub.mean()*(1-b_sub.mean())/peek)
        if se_p > 0:
            z_p = diff / se_p
            p_p = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_p)))
            if p_p < 0.05:
                rejected = True
                break   # stop the experiment early, as the boss wants

    if rejected:
        false_positives += 1

fpr = false_positives / n_sims
print(f"Nominal false positive rate (single test): 5.0%")
print(f"Empirical false positive rate (10 peeks):  {fpr*100:.1f}%")
Nominal false positive rate (single test): 5.0%
Empirical false positive rate (10 peeks):  19.4%

The result is stark. A single properly-timed test keeps the false positive rate at its nominal 5%. But peeking 10 times — and stopping as soon as any peek is significant — inflates that rate to around 19%. That means roughly one in four experiments will declare a winner that doesn’t actually exist, simply because we kept looking.

The practical lesson: decide your sample size in advance, run the experiment to completion, and look at the results once. If you genuinely need to peek (e.g., to catch a catastrophic failure), use methods designed for sequential testing — such as sequential probability ratio tests or alpha-spending functions — that maintain valid error control across multiple looks. Classical frequentist tests, run as described in this post, are calibrated for a single look only.

Source Code
---
title: "Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up"
subtitle: "Simulating Key Ideas from Classical Frequentist Statistics"
author: Hari Chandana Vuppu
date: 2026-04-15
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    theme: cosmo
    highlight-style: github
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
    df-print: default
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.api as sm
from IPython.display import HTML, display

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

BLUE   = "#4C72B0"
ORANGE = "#DD8452"
GREY   = "#8C8C8C"
GREEN  = "#55A868"
RED    = "#C44E52"

plt.rcParams.update({
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.titleweight": "bold",
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.20,
    "font.size": 11,
})


def percent_formatter(decimals=0):
    return mticker.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=decimals)


def display_table(df, caption, fmt=None):
    styler = df.style.hide(axis="index")
    if fmt is not None:
        styler = styler.format(fmt)
    styler = styler.set_caption(caption).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "caption", "props": [("caption-side", "top"), ("font-size", "1.05em"), ("font-weight", "bold")]},
        {"selector": "th", "props": [("text-align", "center"), ("background-color", "#f3f4f6")]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [("text-align", "center")]},
    ])
    display(HTML(styler.to_html()))
```

## Introduction

Suppose you run a small online publication and the homepage includes a newsletter sign-up box.
The box works, but not brilliantly, and you suspect the wording of the prompt might be part of
why. You draft two versions:

- **CTA A:** *"Sign up for our newsletter here!"*
- **CTA B:** *"Stay in the loop — join our newsletter today!"*

They are similar messages, but not identical. One is direct and plain; the other sounds more
conversational and urgency-driven. The business question is simple: **does this wording change
behavior enough to matter?**

That is exactly what an **A/B test** is for. We randomly assign visitors so that one group sees
CTA A and another sees CTA B, then compare the fraction of visitors who sign up. Randomization is
what makes the comparison credible: in expectation, the two groups are alike except for the CTA
shown to them.

This post uses one stylized A/B test to illustrate several core ideas from classical frequentist
statistics:

- the **Law of Large Numbers**,
- the **bootstrap**,
- the **Central Limit Theorem**,
- **hypothesis testing**,
- the connection between a two-group comparison and **regression**, and
- the danger of **peeking** before an experiment is complete.

The goal is not only to get an answer, but to understand *why* the answer is trustworthy.

## The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem

For each visitor, the outcome is binary: they either subscribe (`1`) or they do not (`0`). That
makes the outcome naturally **Bernoulli**. Let

- $\pi_A$ = probability of signing up after seeing CTA A,
- $\pi_B$ = probability of signing up after seeing CTA B.

The estimand we care about is the difference in conversion rates,

$$
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B.
$$

If $\theta > 0$, CTA A converts better. If $\theta < 0$, CTA B converts better. If $\theta = 0$,
then word choice did not change the sign-up rate in any meaningful way.

In practice, we never observe $\pi_A$ or $\pi_B$ directly. We observe samples, and from those
samples we construct an estimator,

$$
\hat\theta = \bar X_A - \bar X_B,
$$

where $\bar X_A$ and $\bar X_B$ are the sample conversion rates in the two groups.

This is a tiny formula with a lot packed into it. The rest of the post is really about three
questions:

1. Does $\hat\theta$ get closer to $\theta$ as the sample grows?
2. How much uncertainty should we attach to a particular estimate?
3. When is a measured difference large enough to count as evidence rather than noise?

## Simulating a Concrete Experiment

In a real experiment we would not know the true probabilities. For teaching purposes, though, it is
useful to set them ourselves so we can see whether the statistical machinery recovers the truth.

Throughout the post, suppose the data-generating process is

$$
\pi_A = 0.22, \qquad \pi_B = 0.18, \qquad \theta = 0.04.
$$

So CTA A truly outperforms CTA B by **4 percentage points**. We simulate 1,000 visitors per group.
That gives us a realistic-sized online experiment without making the code cumbersome.

```{python}
#| echo: true
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
n_per_group = 1_000

A = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n_per_group)
B = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n_per_group)

theta_hat = A.mean() - B.mean()
relative_lift = theta_hat / B.mean()

summary_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["CTA A", "CTA B"],
    "Visitors": [n_per_group, n_per_group],
    "Sign-ups": [A.sum(), B.sum()],
    "Conversion rate": [A.mean(), B.mean()],
})

display_table(
    summary_df,
    "Observed outcomes in the simulated A/B test",
    fmt={"Visitors": "{:,}", "Sign-ups": "{:,}", "Conversion rate": "{:.1%}"},
)

print(f"Estimated difference in conversion rates: {theta_hat:.2%}")
print(f"Relative lift of CTA A over CTA B:       {relative_lift:.1%}")
```

Even before formal inference, the basic story is visible: CTA A converts better in this simulated
sample. But one sample alone cannot tell us whether the estimate is stable or whether we were just
lucky. That is where the Law of Large Numbers enters.

## The Law of Large Numbers

The **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** says that sample averages converge to their population averages
as the sample size grows. In this setting, it implies that the sample conversion rates
$\bar X_A$ and $\bar X_B$ should settle down near $\pi_A$ and $\pi_B$, so their difference
$\hat\theta$ should settle near the true effect $\theta = 0.04$.

A clean way to visualize that idea is to track the running difference in conversion rates as more
visitors arrive.

```{python}
#| echo: true
running_A = np.cumsum(A) / np.arange(1, n_per_group + 1)
running_B = np.cumsum(B) / np.arange(1, n_per_group + 1)
running_theta = running_A - running_B
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Running estimate of the treatment effect. Early results are volatile, but the estimate stabilizes as more visitors are observed."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))
ax.plot(np.arange(1, n_per_group + 1), running_theta, color=BLUE, lw=2, label=r"Running $\hat\theta$")
ax.axhline(pi_A - pi_B, color=RED, ls="--", lw=2, label=r"True $\theta = 4\%$")
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.5, label="No effect")
ax.set_xlim(1, n_per_group)
ax.set_xlabel("Visitors observed per group")
ax.set_ylabel("Difference in conversion rates")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(percent_formatter(0))
ax.set_title("Law of Large Numbers in an A/B Test")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

This figure captures an important practical truth about experimentation: **small samples are noisy**.
Early in the test, the running estimate jumps around enough that a manager checking too soon could
convince themselves that either CTA is winning. Once the sample gets larger, those swings dampen and
the estimate becomes much more stable.

The LLN does not promise that every intermediate estimate is good. It promises that, with enough
observations, the estimator becomes reliable.

## Bootstrap Standard Errors

Stability in the long run is reassuring, but practitioners usually care about a fixed, completed
experiment. We therefore need a way to answer a more immediate question: **how uncertain is this
particular estimate?**

One flexible answer is the **bootstrap**. We repeatedly resample from the observed data *with
replacement*, recompute the estimate each time, and use the resulting distribution to approximate
how much $\hat\theta$ would vary across repeated samples.

```{python}
#| echo: true
n_boot = 2_000
boot_estimates = np.empty(n_boot)

for i in range(n_boot):
    A_star = rng.choice(A, size=n_per_group, replace=True)
    B_star = rng.choice(B, size=n_per_group, replace=True)
    boot_estimates[i] = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()

se_boot = boot_estimates.std(ddof=1)
se_analytic = np.sqrt(A.mean() * (1 - A.mean()) / n_per_group + B.mean() * (1 - B.mean()) / n_per_group)

ci_normal = np.array([theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot, theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot])
ci_percentile = np.quantile(boot_estimates, [0.025, 0.975])

ci_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Method": ["Bootstrap SE", "Analytical SE", "95% CI (normal bootstrap)", "95% CI (percentile bootstrap)"],
    "Result": [
        f"{se_boot:.4f}",
        f"{se_analytic:.4f}",
        f"({ci_normal[0]:.2%}, {ci_normal[1]:.2%})",
        f"({ci_percentile[0]:.2%}, {ci_percentile[1]:.2%})",
    ],
})

display_table(ci_df, "Uncertainty estimates for the treatment effect")
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Bootstrap distribution of the estimated treatment effect. The spread of the histogram reflects the estimator's sampling uncertainty."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 4.5))
ax.hist(boot_estimates, bins=40, color=BLUE, alpha=0.80, edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(theta_hat, color=RED, ls="--", lw=2, label=r"Observed $\hat\theta$")
ax.axvline(ci_percentile[0], color=ORANGE, ls=":", lw=2, label="95% percentile CI")
ax.axvline(ci_percentile[1], color=ORANGE, ls=":", lw=2)
ax.axvline(pi_A - pi_B, color=GREEN, ls="-", lw=2, label=r"True $\theta$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"Bootstrapped $\hat\theta^*$")
ax.set_ylabel("Count")
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(percent_formatter(0))
ax.set_title("Bootstrap Distribution of the Estimated Effect")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The bootstrap and analytical standard errors are very close, which is what we would hope to see.
That agreement gives us confidence that our estimated uncertainty is not an artifact of one method.
The confidence interval also stays away from zero, which already hints that the effect is not just
sampling noise.

## The Central Limit Theorem

The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** explains why Normal-based inference is so useful in A/B tests.
Even though each individual outcome is Bernoulli, the sampling distribution of the estimator
$\hat\theta = \bar X_A - \bar X_B$ becomes approximately Normal when the sample size is large.

To make that visible, we repeatedly simulate fresh experiments at several sample sizes and examine
the resulting distribution of $\hat\theta$.

```{python}
#| echo: true
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
n_reps = 1_500
sim_estimates = {}

for n_sim in sample_sizes:
    a_draws = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, size=(n_reps, n_sim))
    b_draws = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, size=(n_reps, n_sim))
    sim_estimates[n_sim] = a_draws.mean(axis=1) - b_draws.mean(axis=1)
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Sampling distributions of the estimated effect at four sample sizes. As the sample grows, the distribution becomes tighter and more bell-shaped."

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 7))
axes = axes.flatten()
x_grid = np.linspace(-0.25, 0.30, 300)

for ax, n_sim in zip(axes, sample_sizes):
    ests = sim_estimates[n_sim]
    se_n = np.sqrt(pi_A * (1 - pi_A) / n_sim + pi_B * (1 - pi_B) / n_sim)

    ax.hist(ests, bins=35, density=True, color=BLUE, alpha=0.70, edgecolor="white")
    ax.plot(x_grid, stats.norm.pdf(x_grid, loc=pi_A - pi_B, scale=se_n), color=RED, lw=2)
    ax.axvline(pi_A - pi_B, color=GREEN, ls="--", lw=1.8)
    ax.set_title(f"n = {n_sim}")
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$\hat\theta$")
    ax.set_ylabel("Density")
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(percent_formatter(0))

fig.suptitle("Central Limit Theorem: The Sampling Distribution Approaches Normality", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

At $n=25$, the distribution is still rough and somewhat asymmetric. By $n=100$, the Normal
approximation is already much better, and by $n=500$ it is hard to distinguish the simulated
histogram from the theoretical bell curve. That is the CLT doing exactly what textbooks promise.

This is why a Bernoulli outcome does not stop us from using familiar large-sample inference tools.
The raw data are not Normal. The **estimator** becomes approximately Normal.

## Hypothesis Testing

With a large-sample Normal approximation in hand, we can run a formal hypothesis test.

- **Null hypothesis:** $H_0: \theta = 0$
- **Alternative hypothesis:** $H_1: \theta \neq 0$

The test statistic is

$$
z = \frac{\hat\theta}{SE(\hat\theta)}.
$$

If the null is true, then large positive or negative values of $z$ should be rare. The p-value
simply measures how rare our observed statistic would be under that null model.

```{python}
#| echo: true
z_stat = theta_hat / se_analytic
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

hypothesis_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": ["Estimated effect", "Standard error", "z-statistic", "Two-sided p-value"],
    "Value": [f"{theta_hat:.2%}", f"{se_analytic:.2%}", f"{z_stat:.2f}", f"{p_value:.4f}"],
})

display_table(
    hypothesis_df,
    "Large-sample hypothesis test for equal conversion rates"
)
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "The observed test statistic falls in the rejection region of the null distribution, so the data provide strong evidence against equal conversion rates."

x = np.linspace(-4.5, 4.5, 500)
crit = 1.96

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))
ax.plot(x, stats.norm.pdf(x), color=GREY, lw=2, label=r"Null distribution: $N(0,1)$")
ax.fill_between(x[x <= -crit], stats.norm.pdf(x[x <= -crit]), color=RED, alpha=0.25)
ax.fill_between(x[x >= crit], stats.norm.pdf(x[x >= crit]), color=RED, alpha=0.25, label="5% rejection regions")
ax.axvline(-crit, color=RED, ls="--", lw=1.5)
ax.axvline(crit, color=RED, ls="--", lw=1.5)
ax.axvline(z_stat, color=BLUE, lw=2.5, label=f"Observed z = {z_stat:.2f}")
ax.set_xlabel("z")
ax.set_ylabel("Density")
ax.set_title("Where the Test Statistic Lands Under the Null")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

In this simulated experiment, the p-value is small enough to reject the null of equal conversion
rates at the conventional 5% level. The data therefore support the claim that CTA A performs
better than CTA B.

Just as important, the effect is not only statistically significant but also practically visible.
The estimated improvement is material, and represents a meaningful relative lift over the baseline.
Whether that is worth implementing would depend on traffic, downstream revenue, and how expensive
the creative change is — but it is large enough to be interesting in a real product setting.

## The Same Comparison as a Regression

There is a useful algebraic fact hiding inside the two-sample test: it is the same comparison you
would get from a simple linear regression.

Create one dataset with a binary outcome $Y_i$ and an indicator $D_i$ that equals 1 for CTA A and
0 for CTA B. Then fit

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i.
$$

In this model:

- $\beta_0$ is the mean outcome for group B,
- $\beta_0 + \beta_1$ is the mean outcome for group A,
- and therefore $\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta$.

So the regression coefficient on the treatment indicator is literally the treatment effect.

```{python}
#| echo: true
Y = np.concatenate([A, B])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n_per_group), np.zeros(n_per_group)])
X = sm.add_constant(D)

reg = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit(cov_type="HC1")

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": ["Point estimate", "Standard error", "Test statistic", "p-value"],
    "Two-group test": [f"{theta_hat:.2%}", f"{se_analytic:.2%}", f"{z_stat:.2f}", f"{p_value:.4f}"],
    "OLS with robust SE": [f"{reg.params[1]:.2%}", f"{reg.bse[1]:.2%}", f"{reg.tvalues[1]:.2f}", f"{reg.pvalues[1]:.4f}"],
})

display_table(
    comparison_df,
    "Two-sample test and regression give the same substantive answer"
)
```

The coefficient estimate is the same because both procedures are computing the same difference in
means. Using heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors in the regression also lines up the
uncertainty calculation with the binary-outcome setting.

This equivalence matters because regression scales gracefully. Once the simple A/B test is written
as a regression, it becomes easy to extend:

- adjust for day-of-week effects,
- test more than two variants,
- estimate separate effects for mobile and desktop users,
- or include additional pre-treatment covariates to improve precision.

So while a two-sample test is a fine starting point, regression is often the more practical working
language for real experimentation.

## The Problem with Peeking

Everything above assumes one crucial discipline: **we decide on a sample size, run the experiment,
and analyze the result once at the end**.

In practice, teams are often tempted to look early and repeatedly. Maybe the dashboard updates in
real time. Maybe a manager wants to stop as soon as one version looks better. This habit is known as
**peeking**, and it breaks the usual interpretation of a 5% significance threshold.

To see why, simulate a world where the null hypothesis is actually true: both CTAs convert at 20%.
Now pretend we peek after every additional 100 visitors per group and stop the moment a significant
result appears.

```{python}
#| echo: true
pi_null = 0.20
n_total = 1_000
peek_at = np.arange(100, n_total + 1, 100)
n_sims = 10_000

A_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, size=(n_sims, n_total))
B_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, size=(n_sims, n_total))

cum_A = A_null.cumsum(axis=1)
cum_B = B_null.cumsum(axis=1)

peek_idx = peek_at - 1
mean_A_peeks = cum_A[:, peek_idx] / peek_at
mean_B_peeks = cum_B[:, peek_idx] / peek_at

diff_peeks = mean_A_peeks - mean_B_peeks
se_peeks = np.sqrt(mean_A_peeks * (1 - mean_A_peeks) / peek_at + mean_B_peeks * (1 - mean_B_peeks) / peek_at)

z_peeks = np.divide(diff_peeks, se_peeks, out=np.zeros_like(diff_peeks), where=se_peeks > 0)
p_peeks = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(np.abs(z_peeks)))
reject_matrix = p_peeks < 0.05

fpr_by_look = np.maximum.accumulate(reject_matrix, axis=1).mean(axis=0)
final_fpr = fpr_by_look[-1]

print(f"False positive rate with one final look only: {reject_matrix[:, -1].mean():.1%}")
print(f"False positive rate with optional stopping:   {final_fpr:.1%}")
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Each additional look at the data increases the chance of a false discovery when no real treatment effect exists."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 4.5))
ax.plot(np.arange(1, len(peek_at) + 1), fpr_by_look, marker="o", color=RED, lw=2)
ax.axhline(0.05, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.5, label="Nominal 5% level")
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(1, len(peek_at) + 1))
ax.set_xlabel("Number of looks at the data")
ax.set_ylabel("Probability of at least one false positive")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(percent_formatter(0))
ax.set_title("Peeking Inflates the Type I Error Rate")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The key lesson is that a 5% test is calibrated for **one planned analysis**, not for a running
sequence of opportunistic checks. By the tenth look, the probability of declaring a winner when no
winner exists has inflated to roughly 20% — far above the intended 5% level.

That is why experienced experimenters pre-register a stopping rule, fix a sample size in advance,
or use methods specifically designed for sequential monitoring. Without those safeguards, the false
positive rate drifts upward and the experiment becomes much easier to fool.

## Takeaways

This single A/B test illustrates much of the logic behind classical frequentist inference.

- The **Law of Large Numbers** explains why sample conversion rates stabilize.
- The **bootstrap** gives a data-driven way to quantify uncertainty.
- The **Central Limit Theorem** justifies Normal-based inference for large samples.
- A **hypothesis test** turns an observed effect into a formal statement about evidence.
- A two-group comparison can be rewritten as a **regression**, which opens the door to richer models.
- **Peeking** invalidates the usual significance threshold unless the analysis is adjusted for it.

For this simulated experiment, the conclusion is straightforward: CTA A outperforms CTA B, and the
estimated improvement is large enough to be both statistically credible and practically interesting.
More importantly, the path to that conclusion matters. Good experimentation is not just about asking
which variant won. It is about designing the test so that the answer can be trusted.
